title: Ranked Search with Multi-Strategy Fallback Pattern (FTS5 → LIKE → Terms)
summary: A robust search pattern that chains three strategies: FTS5 full-text search (fast, relevance-ranked) → single-pattern LIKE (for exact phrase match) → multi-term LIKE with OR clauses (for individual term matching). Results are deduplicated by chunk_id, scored using a configurable scoring function (section priority + evidence level + signal strength + term coverage + exact match bonuses), then ranked and truncated. The pattern includes a ranked merge algorithm that guarantees at least N results per query before filling remaining slots with best-scoring results across all queries, enforcing result diversity.
why_reusable: Single-strategy search (FTS5-only or LIKE-only) fails on edge cases. The three-strategy chain with fallbacks handles special characters FTS5 can't parse, multi-word queries, and Chinese/English mixed content. The ranked merge with per-query floor is directly applicable to any multi-query search system.
adapter_target: Generic search service — extract search_fts + search_like + search_like_terms + chunk_score + ranked_merge into a reusable SearchEngine class.
implementation_notes: FTS5 first: MATCH query, catches well-formed terms; falls through on OperationalError
LIKE second: single %pattern% for the full normalized query
Multi-term LIKE third: one LIKE clause per term, OR-connected, for partial matches
Deduplication: dict keyed by chunk_id removes duplicates from combined FTS5+LIKE+terms results
Scoring: base score from query_index + section priority + evidence level + signal strength + term coverage
Exact match bonuses: record_id/chunk_id match (12pts), prompt_tags match (8pts), haystack term match (3pts)
Ranked merge: for each query, take floor N results → then fill remaining with best global scores
tests_required: Unit test: FTS5 returns results for valid query
Unit test: LIKE fallback works when FTS5 fails on special characters
Unit test: multi-term LIKE handles CJK characters
Unit test: deduplication removes repeated chunk_ids
Unit test: scoring reflects section priority and evidence level
Unit test: ranked merge preserves per-query floor
Integration test: combined strategies return results even when FTS5 is unavailable
risks: LIKE with %pattern% on large tables without FTS index can be very slow — must cap candidate limits
FTS5 MATCH syntax requires careful query construction to avoid syntax errors
Multi-term LIKE with many terms generates many OR clauses — SQLite has a 1000-parameter limit
Scoring weights are hand-tuned and may not generalize to other domains
do_not_do: Do not run LIKE without a LIMIT clause — it will scan the entire table
Do not pass raw user input to FTS5 MATCH without sanitization
Do not assume FTS5 is always available — always have a LIKE fallback
Do not skip deduplication when combining results from multiple strategies
notes: Extracted from DataBase backend_api/app/main.py search functions and scoring logic. The pattern of FTS5→LIKE→terms with ranked merge is the core retrieval engine powering all domain searches.
